schools.summary: The first demand to Pharaoh - 'Let my people go': 'and afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness' — Moses and Aaron, having gathered the elders (Exodus 4:29-31), going in to Pharaoh with God's message (the first demand, the bold approach, the two brothers before the great king, the embassy of the LORD); the great word — 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness': Moses delivering God's command, 'Let my people go' — the demand that runs through the whole contest (Exodus 7:16; 8:1, 20; 9:1; 10:3), God claiming Israel as 'my people' (his by covenant, not Pharaoh's slaves), and requiring their release to serve (worship) him (the authority of the demand, 'Thus saith the LORD,' the prophetic embassy, the word of the Sovereign to the proud king, the purpose, to hold a feast, to worship and sacrifice, the service of God the end of the deliverance — redeemed to serve, Exodus 4:23, the people freed that they might worship, the keynote of the Exodus); 'that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness' (the immediate request, a three days' journey to sacrifice, v.3, the worship of God the purpose, the deliverance unto service, the redeemed people called to serve their God, not Pharaoh, the demand that began the contest) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness
the first demand to Pharaoh - 'Let my people go' (the crux of the deliverance summons)
God claiming Israel as 'my people' (his by covenant), demanding their release to worship him (Exodus 7:16)
the deliverance unto service - redeemed to serve God, not Pharaoh (Exodus 4:23)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's proud defiance of the LORD: 'and Pharaoh said, Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go' — Pharaoh's words — 'Who is the LORD (Jehovah), that I should obey his voice to let Israel go?': Pharaoh contemptuously demanding who this 'LORD' (Jehovah, the God of the Hebrews) was, that he, the great king of Egypt, should obey him (the proud defiance, the haughty contempt of God by the worldly potentate, the challenge to God's authority, Pharaoh, himself accounted a god, refusing to acknowledge the LORD, the arrogance that set the proud king against the Almighty, the contest joined, the question 'Who is the LORD?' that the plagues would answer, Pharaoh learning, to his ruin, who the LORD is, Exodus 7:5, 17; 9:16; 14:18, 'the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD'); 'I know not the LORD' (Pharaoh's ignorance, and willful refusal to acknowledge Jehovah, not that he was ignorant of the Hebrews' God by report, but he 'knew not' in the sense of refusing to own his authority, as he 'knew not Joseph,' Exodus 1:8, the practical atheism of the proud, acknowledging not God, Psalm 14:1, the heart that says, 'who is the LORD?', Proverbs 30:9, the defiant unbelief); 'neither will I let Israel go' (Pharaoh's flat refusal, the hardened will against God, the defiance that would be broken by the plagues, the providence, God's purpose to show his power on the proud king, Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17, 'for this same purpose have I raised thee up, that I might shew my power in thee,' the contest between the proud king and the Almighty begun) — Calvin, Clarke, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD? I know not the LORD' display?

sides.position: The proud potentate's contemptuous defiance of God's authority - the practical atheism that acknowledges not God (Psalm 14:1), the willful refusal (as 'knew not Joseph,' Exodus 1:8); the question the plagues would answer (Pharaoh learning who the LORD is, Exodus 7:5; 14:18), the king raised up that God might show his power (Romans 9:17)

themes: And Pharaoh said, Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go
Pharaoh's proud defiance of the LORD (the crux of the denial of God)
the proud potentate's contemptuous denial of God's authority, the practical atheism (Psalm 14:1; Exodus 1:8)
the question the plagues would answer, the king raised up that God might show his power (Exodus 7:5; Romans 9:17)

schools.summary: Moses and Aaron pressing the request to sacrifice: 'and they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go, we pray thee, three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the LORD our God; lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword' — Moses and Aaron, meeting Pharaoh's defiance (v.2), pressing the request: the God of the Hebrews had met with them (at the bush, Exodus 3), and required them to go three days' journey to sacrifice (the renewed appeal, the modest request, a journey to worship, the immediate demand, behind which lay the full deliverance); the request (a three days' journey to sacrifice, away from Egypt where their worship would offend the Egyptians, Exodus 8:26) — the immediate demand (the test of Pharaoh, whether he would grant even this, the worship of God the purpose); 'lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword' (the warning, that God might punish them if they failed to worship, the reverent fear of God, and, the witnesses note, a hint to Pharaoh of the judgments his refusal might bring, the appeal to Pharaoh's interest, lest the judgment fall on Egypt too, the plea pressed) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go... three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the LORD our God; lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword
Moses and Aaron pressing the request to sacrifice
the renewed appeal - the God of the Hebrews requiring their worship (Exodus 3; 8:26)
the warning of judgment pressed on the defiant king

schools.summary: Pharaoh accusing Moses of idling the people: 'and the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens' — Pharaoh accusing Moses and Aaron of 'letting' (hindering, distracting) the people from their labor, and bidding them get back to their burdens (the contemptuous dismissal, the king regarding the request only as a slackening of the slaves' work, the refusal of the demand); Pharaoh's charge (that Moses and Aaron were distracting the slaves from their work) — the king's view (the worship-request a mere pretext for idleness, v.17, the contempt for the demand); 'get you unto your burdens' (Pharaoh ordering the people, or Moses and Aaron, reckoned among the slaves, back to their forced labor, the dismissal of the embassy, the refusal that would harden into the increased burden, vv.6-9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens
Pharaoh accusing Moses of idling the people
the contemptuous dismissal, the request regarded as mere idleness (v.17)
the prelude to the heavier burden (vv.6-9)

schools.summary: Pharaoh grudging the people any rest: 'and Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens' — Pharaoh complaining that the people (the Israelites, now many, Exodus 1:7) were being given rest from their labor by Moses and Aaron's intervention (the grudging of any respite, the king's view, the slaves should be kept ever at work, not given a holiday to worship, the hardness of the oppressor); 'the people... are many' (the Israelites grown numerous, Exodus 1:7) — the very increase that Pharaoh feared (Exodus 1:9, the multitude he would keep enslaved); 'ye make them rest from their burdens' (Pharaoh grudging the slaves any rest, the request to worship seen as mere idleness, the hardness, the oppressor begrudging even a day's worship, the providence, the grudge that would harden into the heavier burden, vv.6-9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens
Pharaoh grudging the people any rest
the hardness of the oppressor begrudging even a day's worship
the very increase he feared (Exodus 1:7, 9), the prelude to the heavier burden

schools.summary: Pharaoh's command to the taskmasters: 'and Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying' — Pharaoh, the same day (in his anger at the demand, vv.1-5), issuing a new command to the taskmasters (the Egyptian slave-drivers) and the officers (the Hebrew foremen, set over the people, the prompt cruelty, the order to increase the burden, vv.7-9, the retaliation for the request); the taskmasters (Egyptian) and officers (Hebrew foremen, v.14) — the chain of command over the forced labor (the instruments of the increased oppression); Pharaoh's command (the same day, in retaliation) — the prompt, vindictive cruelty (punishing the people for Moses' demand, the order to make the bondage harder, vv.7-9, the providence, the affliction worsening at the first attempt at deliverance, the trial of Israel's faith) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying
Pharaoh's command to the taskmasters
the prompt, vindictive cruelty in retaliation for the demand
the affliction worsening at the first attempt at deliverance (vv.7-9)

schools.summary: The order to withhold straw from the brickmakers: 'ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves' — Pharaoh ordering that the straw (chopped straw, mixed into the Nile-mud to bind the bricks) no longer be supplied to the Israelites; instead they must gather their own straw (stubble, v.12), yet make the same number of bricks (v.8, the cruel device, doubling the labor while keeping the quota, the increased bondage); the straw (used in the Egyptian brick-making, mixed with the clay) — the material now withheld (the labor of gathering it added to the labor of brick-making); 'let them go and gather straw for themselves' (the people sent to gather their own straw, scattered through the fields, v.12, the cruel multiplying of the toil, the same quota, less means, the affliction worsened, the deliverance attempt bringing, at first, harder bondage, the providence, the trial that would make the deliverance the more glorious) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves
the order to withhold straw from the brickmakers
the cruel device - the same quota with less means (v.8)
the affliction worsened at the first attempt at deliverance (v.12)

schools.summary: The unchanged quota and Pharaoh's charge of idleness: 'and the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof: for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice to our God' — the full daily quota of bricks still required, though the straw was withheld (v.7, the cruel impossibility, the same number with double the labor, the design to crush the people); the quota unchanged (no reduction allowed) — the breaking burden (the cruelty of the unattainable demand); 'for they be idle; therefore they cry, Let us go and sacrifice to our God' (Pharaoh's charge that the people were idle, and so had leisure to think of worship, the slander, the worship-request branded as the pretext of idle slaves, the contempt for the service of God, the worldly view that counts worship idleness, the hardening of the oppressor, turning the holy desire into an accusation) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: And the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof: for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice to our God
the unchanged quota and Pharaoh's charge of idleness
the cruel impossibility - the same number with double the labor
the worship-request slandered as the pretext of idle slaves (v.17)

schools.summary: Pharaoh laying heavier work to silence the demand: 'let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein; and let them not regard vain words' — Pharaoh ordering heavier work laid on the Israelites, that they might be kept toiling (the design, to crush their spirit and leave them no leisure to listen to Moses, the increased bondage, the cruelty meant to break the people); Pharaoh's purpose — the heavier work designed to occupy and crush the people, so that they would have no time or heart to think of deliverance (the cruel policy, the more the deliverance was sought, the heavier the bondage grew, the providence — the strange and trying way of God, that the first effect of Moses' coming was not relief but harder bondage, the trial of Israel's faith, the deliverance seeming, at first, to make things worse, that the people might learn to trust God and not the means, and that the final deliverance might be the more glorious, by God's hand alone, against the hardened oppressor, the lesson — God's deliverances often begin with deeper trouble, the darkest hour before the dawn); 'let them not regard vain words' (Pharaoh branding Moses' message, the word of God, 'Let my people go,' v.1, as 'vain, or false, words,' the contempt for God's word, the proud king counting the divine promise empty talk, as Sennacherib, 2 Kings 18:20, the design to keep the people from heeding the promise of deliverance, the hardening that despised God's word) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the heavier burden at the first demand display?

sides.position: The strange providence that the first effect of the deliverer's coming was harder bondage - the trial of Israel's faith (the deliverance seeming to make things worse, that the people might trust God alone and the final deliverance be the more glorious); God's deliverances often begin with deeper trouble

themes: Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein; and let them not regard vain words
Pharaoh laying heavier work to silence the demand (the crux of the worsened affliction)
the strange providence - the first effect of the deliverer's coming was harder bondage, the trial of faith
Pharaoh's contempt of God's word as 'vain words' (2 Kings 18:20)

schools.summary: The taskmasters publishing Pharaoh's cruel order: 'and the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw' — the taskmasters and officers going out and announcing Pharaoh's order to the people: no more straw would be given (the cruel decree published, the burden laid, vv.7-9 carried out, the people to gather their own straw yet make the full quota); 'Thus saith Pharaoh' — the proud king's decree (a dark echo of 'Thus saith the LORD,' v.1, the word of the oppressor set against the word of God, the announcement of the cruelty, the burden imposed); the taskmasters publishing the order — the cruel decree carried out (no straw, full quota, the affliction worsened, the providence, the trial of Israel under the hardened bondage) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Gill.

themes: And the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw
the taskmasters publishing Pharaoh's cruel order
'Thus saith Pharaoh' - the word of the oppressor set against the word of God (v.1)
the cruel decree carried out, the affliction worsened (vv.7-9)

schools.summary: The people sent for straw with no reduction of work: 'go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished' — the people sent to find straw wherever they could, yet without any reduction of their work (the full quota of bricks still required, the cruel impossibility, the burden doubled, the gathering of straw added, the quota unchanged, the affliction at its height); the people sent to scour the land for straw (stubble, v.12) — the added, scattered labor (the burden multiplied); 'yet not ought of your work shall be diminished' (the quota unchanged, no allowance for the added labor, the breaking, impossible demand, the affliction worsened, the providence, the trial of the people under the cruel bondage) — Gill.

themes: Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished
the people sent for straw with no reduction of work
the impossible burden - the gathering added, the quota unchanged
the affliction at its height (v.12)

schools.summary: The people scattered to gather stubble: 'so the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw' — the Israelites scattered through all Egypt, gathering stubble (the short, dry stalks left after harvest) in place of the straw now withheld (the wearisome, scattered labor, the burden multiplied, the search for stubble added to the brick-making, the affliction); the people scattered (searching far and wide for stubble) — the laborious gathering (the burden of the search); 'to gather stubble instead of straw' (the stubble, an inferior substitute for the proper straw, gathered with great toil, the cruel multiplying of the labor, the affliction worsened, the providence, the trial of the people, the bondage at its height before the deliverance) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: So the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw
the people scattered to gather stubble
the wearisome search added to the brick-making
the affliction at its height before the deliverance

schools.summary: The taskmasters driving the people to the full quota: 'and the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw' — the taskmasters driving (hurrying, pressing) the people to fulfill the full daily quota of bricks, just as when straw was supplied (the relentless driving, the impossible demand, the same tasks, no straw, the cruelty pressing the exhausted people); the taskmasters' driving (the relentless pressure, the slave-drivers urging the people on) — the harsh cruelty (the impossible demand pressed); 'Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw' (the full quota demanded, as if straw were still given, the cruel, impossible standard, the affliction, the people unable to meet the quota, vv.14, the providence, the bondage at its bitterest) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Ellicott.

themes: And the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw
the taskmasters driving the people to the full quota
the relentless driving, the impossible demand (the same tasks, no straw)
the bondage at its bitterest (v.14)

schools.summary: The Hebrew officers beaten for the unmet quota: 'and the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?' — the Hebrew officers (foremen, set over the people by the Egyptian taskmasters) beaten because the people, lacking straw, could not make the full quota (the cruelty falling on the foremen, the impossible demand punished with stripes, the suffering of the leaders for the people's unmet quota); the beating (the Hebrew foremen scourged for the shortfall) — the cruelty of the impossible demand (the innocent foremen suffering for what they could not prevent, the affliction); 'Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?' (the demand, why the quota was not met, as before, the impossible standard, the same quota, no straw, the unjust punishment, the affliction, the bondage at its bitterest, driving the officers to appeal to Pharaoh, vv.15-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

themes: And the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten...
the Hebrew officers beaten for the unmet quota
the innocent foremen scourged for the impossible demand
the bondage at its bitterest, driving them to appeal (vv.15-16)

schools.summary: The officers appealing to Pharaoh: 'then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?' — the Hebrew foremen, beaten (v.14), coming and appealing to Pharaoh himself, crying out at the injustice of the impossible demand (the appeal to the king, not knowing the order was Pharaoh's own, the cry of the oppressed, the protest at the cruelty); the officers' appeal (to the king, seeking relief) — the cry against the injustice (the appeal to the very source of the cruelty, unknowing, the protest of the wronged); 'Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?' (the officers' protest at the unjust dealing, the impossible demand, the beating, the appeal to Pharaoh's justice, in vain, the order being his own, v.17, the cry of the oppressed to the oppressor) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?
the officers appealing to Pharaoh
the cry of the oppressed against the injustice
the appeal to the very source of the cruelty, unknowing (v.17)

schools.summary: The officers laying the fault on Pharaoh's people: 'there is no straw given unto thy servants, and they say to us, Make brick: and, behold, thy servants are beaten; but the fault is in thine own people' — the officers protesting: no straw was given, yet brick was demanded, and they were beaten for the shortfall (the fault lay not in them but in Pharaoh's own people, the taskmasters, demanding the impossible, the appeal to justice, the laying of the blame on the Egyptian oppressors); the impossible demand (no straw, yet brick required) — the injustice protested (the cruelty of the Egyptian taskmasters); 'the fault is in thine own people' (the officers laying the fault on the Egyptian taskmasters, Pharaoh's own people, demanding the impossible, the appeal to Pharaoh's justice, not knowing the order was his, the protest of the wronged, in vain, v.17) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: There is no straw given unto thy servants, and they say to us, Make brick: and, behold, thy servants are beaten; but the fault is in thine own people
the officers laying the fault on Pharaoh's people
the injustice - no straw, yet brick demanded, and beaten for the shortfall
the appeal to Pharaoh's justice, in vain (v.17)

schools.summary: Pharaoh branding the people idle: 'but he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD' — Pharaoh, rejecting the officers' appeal, twice charging them with idleness ('ye are idle, ye are idle') — the contemptuous accusation (the people had too much leisure, hence their desire to go and worship, the dismissal of the appeal, the hardening of the king); the doubled charge of idleness — the slander (the worldly view that counts the worship of God idleness, the desire to serve God a mark of having too little to do, the contempt for the service of God, the hardening of the oppressor); 'therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD' (Pharaoh branding the holy desire, to worship God, v.1, as the pretext of idleness, the contempt of God's service, the worldly heart that sees devotion as laziness, the hardening that despised both God and his worship, the refusal that pressed the bondage harder, v.18) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: But he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD
Pharaoh branding the people idle
the holy desire to worship branded as the pretext of idleness (v.1)
the worldly contempt of God's service and devotion

schools.summary: Pharaoh's final refusal and the impossible demand: 'go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks' — Pharaoh dismissing the officers with the impossible demand: no straw, yet the full quota of bricks (the flat refusal of relief, the hardening of the king, the bondage at its bitterest, the same quota, no means); the refusal of straw (the means withheld) — the cruelty unrelenting (the impossible demand confirmed); 'yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks' (the full quota still required, no straw, full number, the breaking, impossible demand, the affliction, the people crushed, the officers in despair, vv.19-21, the providence, the bondage at its height before the deliverance) — Gill.

themes: Go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks
Pharaoh's final refusal and the impossible demand
the unrelenting cruelty - no straw, yet the full quota
the bondage at its height before the deliverance (vv.19-21)

schools.summary: The officers seeing their desperate plight: 'and the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task' — the Hebrew foremen seeing they were in a desperate plight (evil case): the quota would not be reduced, the straw not given, and they themselves beaten (the despair, no relief, the impossible demand fixed, the bitterness of the bondage, the plight that turned their distress against Moses, vv.20-21); the officers' despair (seeing no way out, the demand impossible, the beating unjust) — the misery of the bondage (the plight that bred their reproach of Moses, v.21); 'Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task' (the quota fixed, no reduction, the impossible demand confirmed, the despair, the officers in evil case, turning to blame Moses and Aaron, vv.20-21, the providence, the trial at its bitterest) — JFB, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task
the officers seeing their desperate plight
the despair of the bondage - the impossible demand fixed, no relief
the plight that turned to blame Moses (vv.20-21)

schools.summary: The officers meeting Moses and Aaron: 'and they met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way, as they came forth from Pharaoh' — the officers, coming out from Pharaoh (their appeal rejected, vv.17-18), meeting Moses and Aaron who stood waiting (perhaps to learn the outcome, the meeting, the occasion of the officers' reproach, v.21, the distress turning against the deliverers); Moses and Aaron standing in the way (waiting to hear the result of the appeal) — the deliverers awaiting the outcome (the meeting with the despairing officers); 'as they came forth from Pharaoh' (the officers coming from the rejected appeal, vv.17-18, the meeting that occasioned the reproach, v.21, the providence, the distress that would drive Moses to God, vv.22-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way, as they came forth from Pharaoh
the officers meeting Moses and Aaron
the despairing officers meeting the deliverers (vv.17-18)
the occasion of the reproach (v.21)

schools.summary: The officers reproaching Moses for their worsened plight: 'and they said unto them, The LORD look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants, to put a sword in their hand to slay us' — the officers, in their distress, reproaching Moses and Aaron, calling on the LORD to judge them (the bitter complaint, blaming the deliverers for the worsened bondage, the reproach of the despairing, turning on the very men sent to deliver them); the charge — Moses and Aaron had made Israel's 'savour' (their name, repute) abhorred (a stench) before Pharaoh (the blame, that the demand had only angered the king and worsened the bondage, the complaint of the oppressed, the deliverance attempt seeming to make things worse, the people's faith failing under the trial); 'to put a sword in their hand to slay us' (the officers fearing the bondage would now destroy them, the sword in Pharaoh's hand, the despair, blaming Moses for their danger, the weakness of faith under the trial, the people, lately believing, Exodus 4:31, now turning against the deliverers when the affliction worsened, the providence, the trial that would drive Moses to God, vv.22-23, and prepare the people for the deliverance by God's hand alone) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And they said unto them, The LORD look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh... to put a sword in their hand to slay us
the officers reproaching Moses for their worsened plight
the despairing people turning on the deliverers when the affliction worsened
the failing of faith under trial (lately believing, Exodus 4:31)

schools.summary: Moses' complaint to God at the worsened affliction: 'and Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?' — Moses, distressed by the people's reproach (v.21) and the worsened bondage, returning to the LORD (in prayer, the recourse to God, the right resort of the troubled servant, carrying his perplexity to God, the laying of the burden before the LORD); Moses' words — 'wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?': Moses complaining (expostulating) to God: why had God dealt so ill with the people (the affliction worsening since Moses came), and why had God sent him (the mission seeming to fail)? — the perplexity of faith (Moses not understanding God's strange way, the deliverance beginning with deeper trouble, the witnesses balancing: the complaint shows the weakness and impatience of Moses' faith, a fault, the murmuring of perplexity, questioning God's dealings, yet it was the complaint of faith, not unbelief — Moses bringing his trouble to God, not casting off the mission, but seeking God in his perplexity, the honest wrestling of the servant who did not yet see God's design, the lesson — faith may be perplexed and complain, yet still hold to God and bring its trouble to him, as the Psalms of lament, Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3); 'why is it that thou hast sent me?' (Moses questioning his mission, the deliverance seeming to fail, the bondage worse, the trial of the deliverer's faith, the strange providence, that God's sending had, at first, worsened things, the perplexity that God would answer with the renewed promise, Exodus 6:1-8, 'now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh,' the faith that, though perplexed, returned to God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How is Moses' complaint to God to be regarded?

sides.position: The complaint of faith, not unbelief - Moses bringing his perplexity to God (as the laments, Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3), the honest wrestling of the servant who did not yet see God's design; though it shows the weakness and impatience of his faith, he held to God and was soon answered (Exodus 6:1-8)

themes: And Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?
Moses' complaint to God at the worsened affliction (the crux of faith perplexed)
the perplexity of faith at God's strange way (the deliverance beginning with deeper trouble)
the complaint of faith, not unbelief - brought to God (Psalm 13:1-2; Habakkuk 1:2-3), soon answered (Exodus 6:1-8)

schools.summary: Moses' lament that no deliverance had come: 'for since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all' — Moses lamenting that since he came to Pharaoh in God's name (v.1), Pharaoh had only done worse evil to the people (the harder bondage, vv.6-18, the deliverance attempt seeming to fail, the perplexity, the mission bringing, at first, more affliction); Moses' lament (the bondage worse since his coming) — the trial of faith (the strange providence, the perplexity of the servant, not seeing God's design); 'neither hast thou delivered thy people at all' (Moses' complaint that God had not delivered at all, no sign of deliverance, only worse bondage, the impatience of faith, expecting a quicker deliverance, yet the complaint brought to God, the honest lament, soon answered, Exodus 6:1, 'now shalt thou see what I will do,' the providence, the deliverance only delayed, not denied, the trial preparing the glorious deliverance by God's hand) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all
Moses' lament that no deliverance had come
the deliverance seeming to fail, the bondage worse since his coming (vv.6-18)
the honest lament of faith brought to God, soon answered (Exodus 6:1)
